laparoscopic cholecystectomy.simpleDefinition: Removal of the gallbladder using a tiny camera and small cuts in your belly, instead of one large opening.
laparoscopic cholecystectomy.analogy: Think of it like the surgeon using a miniature flashlight and tools on long sticks, guided by a TV screen — no need to open you up.

general anesthesia.simpleDefinition: Medicine given through an IV that puts you into a deep, controlled sleep so you feel no pain during surgery.
general anesthesia.analogy: Like being put into a dreamless sleep that doctors can turn on and off safely.
general anesthesia.quizHint: What does general anesthesia do to you during surgery?

myocardial infarction.simpleDefinition: A heart attack — when blood flow to part of the heart muscle is blocked, causing damage.
myocardial infarction.analogy: Like a garden hose getting kinked — the part of the garden it feeds starts to die.
myocardial infarction.quizHint: A myocardial infarction is another name for what common emergency?

hemorrhage.simpleDefinition: Heavy or uncontrolled bleeding, either inside or outside the body.
hemorrhage.analogy: Like a pipe bursting — blood escapes from where it should stay contained.
hemorrhage.quizHint: Hemorrhage refers to what type of medical complication?

informed consent.simpleDefinition: Your legal agreement that you understand the procedure, its risks, and alternatives before it happens.
informed consent.analogy: Like signing a contract after reading it — not just because someone handed you a pen.
informed consent.quizHint: What does giving informed consent mean you have done?

postoperative.simpleDefinition: The period of time after your surgery while your body is healing.
postoperative.analogy: The 'after' chapter of your surgery story.
postoperative.quizHint: Postoperative complications are problems that happen when — before or after surgery?

intravenous.simpleDefinition: Delivered directly into a vein, usually through a thin tube (IV line) in your arm.
intravenous.analogy: A tiny highway directly into your bloodstream.
intravenous.quizHint: Intravenous medication is given through what part of the body?

sutures.simpleDefinition: Stitches used to close a wound or surgical incision so it heals properly.
sutures.analogy: Like sewing fabric back together, but on skin or tissue.
sutures.quizHint: Sutures are used to do what to a wound?

biopsy.simpleDefinition: Removing a small sample of tissue from your body so it can be examined under a microscope.
biopsy.analogy: Like taking a tiny soil sample to test before deciding what to plant.
biopsy.quizHint: Why is a biopsy performed on body tissue?

pulmonary embolism.simpleDefinition: A blood clot that travels to your lungs and blocks blood flow there, which can be life-threatening.
pulmonary embolism.analogy: Like a traffic jam that forms somewhere else but ends up blocking a critical highway — the ones supplying your lungs.
pulmonary embolism.quizHint: A pulmonary embolism is a clot that travels to which organ?

sedation.simpleDefinition: Medicine that makes you feel calm, drowsy, or partially asleep during a procedure — lighter than full anesthesia.
sedation.analogy: Like the feeling of drifting off on a very relaxing afternoon nap, but controlled by your doctor.
sedation.quizHint: Sedation is used to make you feel what during a procedure?

intubation.simpleDefinition: Placing a breathing tube down your throat so a machine can breathe for you while you are under general anesthesia.
intubation.analogy: Like a snorkel that connects your airway to a machine that breathes on your behalf.
intubation.quizHint: Why is an intubation tube placed during some surgeries?

incision.simpleDefinition: A cut made by the surgeon to access the area being operated on.
incision.analogy: Like opening a zipper to get inside — controlled, precise, and closed back up afterward.

catheter.simpleDefinition: A thin, flexible tube inserted into your body to drain fluids — most commonly urine from your bladder during surgery.
catheter.analogy: Like a small drain pipe placed temporarily to keep things clear while you can't move around.
catheter.quizHint: What does a catheter do inside the body?

thrombosis.simpleDefinition: A blood clot that forms inside a blood vessel and blocks blood from flowing through it normally.
thrombosis.analogy: Like a blockage forming in a pipe that stops water from getting through.
thrombosis.quizHint: Thrombosis refers to the formation of what inside a blood vessel?

edema.simpleDefinition: Swelling caused by fluid building up in your body's tissues, often in the legs or around the surgical area.
edema.analogy: Like a sponge that has absorbed too much water and puffed up.
edema.quizHint: What causes the swelling known as edema?

abscess.simpleDefinition: A pocket of pus that forms in your body as a result of an infection — it may need to be drained.
abscess.analogy: Like a bubble of infection your body tries to wall off to stop it from spreading.
abscess.quizHint: An abscess is a pocket of what inside the body?

hypertension.simpleDefinition: High blood pressure — when the force of blood pushing through your arteries is consistently too high.
hypertension.analogy: Like water flowing through a hose at dangerously high pressure, straining the walls.
hypertension.quizHint: Hypertension is the medical term for what common condition?

anesthesiologist.simpleDefinition: The doctor who gives you anesthesia and monitors your breathing, heart rate, and safety throughout the entire surgery.
anesthesiologist.analogy: Like the co-pilot of the operating room — focused entirely on keeping you safe and unconscious at the right level.
anesthesiologist.quizHint: What is the main job of an anesthesiologist during surgery?

complication.simpleDefinition: An unexpected problem that arises during or after surgery, beyond the normal expected outcome.
complication.analogy: Like a detour that appears on a road trip — not planned for, but something the medical team is trained to handle.
complication.quizHint: What does it mean when a surgical complication occurs?

prognosis.simpleDefinition: Your doctor's best prediction of how your condition will develop and what your recovery will likely look like.
prognosis.analogy: Like a weather forecast for your health — based on evidence, not a guarantee.
prognosis.quizHint: What does a prognosis tell you about your condition?

pathology.simpleDefinition: The study of disease — in surgery, this usually means sending removed tissue to a lab to be examined for signs of illness.
pathology.analogy: Like sending a soil sample to a lab to find out exactly what's in it.
pathology.quizHint: Why is tissue sent to pathology after some surgeries?

sterile field.simpleDefinition: The completely germ-free area maintained around the surgical site to prevent infection during the operation.
sterile field.analogy: Like a clean room in a lab — everything inside it has been decontaminated and nothing unclean is allowed in.
sterile field.quizHint: Why is a sterile field maintained during surgery?

consent form.simpleDefinition: A legal document you sign to confirm you understand the procedure, its risks, benefits, and alternatives before agreeing to it.
consent form.analogy: Like signing a contract after reading it — your signature means you understood and agreed.
consent form.quizHint: What does signing a consent form confirm?

local anesthesia.simpleDefinition: Medicine injected to numb just one small area of your body so you feel no pain there, while staying fully awake.
local anesthesia.analogy: Like turning off the pain signal in one small room of a building while the rest stays fully lit.
local anesthesia.quizHint: How is local anesthesia different from general anesthesia?

vital signs.simpleDefinition: Key measurements that show how your body is functioning — heart rate, blood pressure, breathing rate, and temperature.
vital signs.analogy: Like the dashboard gauges in a car that tell you if everything is running normally.
vital signs.quizHint: Which of the following is one of the four main vital signs?

inflammation.simpleDefinition: Your body's natural response to injury or infection — the area becomes red, warm, swollen, and painful as your immune system responds.
inflammation.analogy: Like your body sounding an alarm and sending repair crews to the site of damage.
inflammation.quizHint: What are the signs that inflammation is occurring in body tissue?

drainage.simpleDefinition: The removal of fluid, pus, or blood from a wound or body cavity, either naturally or through a tube placed by a doctor.
drainage.analogy: Like unclogging a backed-up sink so fluid can flow out instead of pooling.
drainage.quizHint: What is the purpose of surgical drainage?

NPO.simpleDefinition: Nothing by mouth — you are not allowed to eat or drink anything before your surgery, usually starting the night before.
NPO.analogy: Like an empty stomach rule before a big physical event — required for your safety under anesthesia.
NPO.quizHint: What does NPO mean you must do before surgery?

discharge.simpleDefinition: When your medical team officially releases you from the hospital or clinic to go home after your procedure.
discharge.analogy: Like being checked out of a hotel — you've been cleared to leave and continue recovery at home.
discharge.quizHint: What does it mean when a patient is discharged from the hospital?
